figures.caption: Figure 1. Faithfulness-Efficiency Trade-off. (a) Baseline methods compromise either efficiency or faithfulness. Our approach simultaneously achieves both faithfulness and efficiency (R2 of predicted vs. actual logit drops). (b) Latency scaling with context length. Unlike the baseline, which exhibits linear cost growth and OOM errors on long traces, our method operates with constant overhead below the real-time threshold (dashed line).
Figure 4. Predicted vs. actual ablation effects. Each point represents a region’s predicted effect versus its ground-truth logprobability drop.

Table 1. Attribution quality comparison. Each cell: (LDS / Top-5 Drop); higher is better. Green: best, cream: second best. We define real-time as ≥10 tokens/sec, exceeding the average human silent reading speed of 238 words/min (≈6 tokens/sec) (Brysbaert, 2019), ensuring users can follow streaming output; VSTREAM (Ours) achieves up to 117× speedup over gradient-based baselines.
Table 2. Cross-task generalization. Each cell: (LDS / Top-5 Drop). Diagonal: in-domain.
Figure 33. Additional qualitative results: Cosmos-R1 on math/science reasoning.

Figure 13. Per-caption attribution concentration vs. CHAIRi (n=2,000 COCO captions, Spearman ρ=0.46, p<.001). Higher concentration during caption generation predicts more severe object hallucination, extending the Fixation finding to open-ended generation.
Figure 29. Additional qualitative results: GLM-4.1V on math/science reasoning.
Figure 23. Additional qualitative results: Qwen3-VL on math/science reasoning.
Figure 34. Additional qualitative results: Cosmos-R1 on general/document/code reasoning.
Table 11. Cross-task generalization for MiMo-VL-7B. Each cell shows (LDS / Top-5 Drop).
Table 12. Cross-task generalization for Cosmos-R1. Each cell shows (LDS / Top-5 Drop).
Figure 38. Additional qualitative results: MiMo-VL on general/document/code reasoning.
Figure 2. Comparison of attribution processes between our method and baselines.

Figure 5. Qualitative comparison on a real-world sample (Qwen3-VL). VSTREAM emits per-step visual attributions alongside the model’s reasoning at near-zero latency, while prior methods only run post-hoc once generation has finished.
Figure 37. Additional qualitative results: MiMo-VL on math/science reasoning.
Table 10. Cross-task generalization for GLM-4.1V-9B-Thinking. Each cell shows (LDS / Top-5 Drop). Diagonal entries (shaded) indicate in-domain performance.

Figure 14. Estimator weight heatmaps w ∈ RL×H across four architectures. All models concentrate weight in early-to-mid layers, suggesting a consistent architectural prior: early layers encode coarse visual-semantic alignment that is most predictive of ablation effects.
Table 4. Key hyperparameters for reproducibility.
Figure 30. Additional qualitative results: GLM-4.1V on general/document/code reasoning.

Figure 3. Overview of our amortized attribution pipeline. (a) Training: We use our semantic region unitization to identify semantic regions of an input image using DINO features and optimize a lightweight estimator to predict causal importance from attention patterns. (b) Inference: Once the estimator is trained, it generalizes to other samples and computes visual attribution in parallel with the model’s token generation.
Figure 22. Additional qualitative results: Qwen3-VL on general/document/code reasoning.
Figure 36. Additional qualitative results: MiMo-VL on general/document/code reasoning.
Figure 31. Additional qualitative results: GLM-4.1V on math/science reasoning.
Figure 26. Additional qualitative results: Qwen3-VL on general/document/code reasoning.

Figure 7. Reasoning trajectories in visual attribution space. Each curve traces the evolution of a region-effect vector across reasoning steps, projected into 3D via PCA, The figure shows views from five different angles (θ). The thinking process begins at the circular point and terminates at the square point. Successful reasoning chains ( orange) follow compact, directed paths that converge toward stable visual grounding. Unsuccessful chains ( purple) exhibit longer, more tortuous trajectories (see purple dashed boxes), reflecting repeated reassignment of visual support across regions.
Figure 15. Generation length distribution across training examples. The distribution is bimodal: short VQA responses (∼400 tokens) and long reasoning traces (∼3,200 tokens).

Figure 16. Faithfulness (R2) by generation-length quintile. No systematic degradation as context length increases, indicating that the estimator generalizes across short VQA and long reasoning traces.
Figure 25. Additional qualitative results: Qwen3-VL on math/science reasoning.
Figure 27. Additional qualitative results: Qwen3-VL on math/science reasoning.

Figure 17. OOD attribution on VQA-RAD (medical radiology). The estimator, trained on natural images only and applied without retraining, correctly localizes kidneys in abdominal CT (top, bottom) and a pacemaker in chest X-ray (middle).
Figure 32. Additional qualitative results: Cosmos-R1 on general/document/code reasoning.

Figure 10. Attribution concentration and early failure detection. (Left) Mean concentration over normalized reasoning steps (mean ± SEM), grouped by outcome type. (Right) Tortuosity-based failure prediction AUC over reasoning progress.
Figure 35. Additional qualitative results: Cosmos-R1 on math/science reasoning.
